glossary.def: The distance from a classifier's decision boundary to the nearest training point of either class. A support vector machine chooses the boundary that makes this distance as large as possible; under canonical scaling the half-width margin equals 1/||w||, so maximizing the margin is the same as minimizing the weight norm.

A training point that lies exactly on the margin (or inside it, for the soft margin) and so has a nonzero dual weight alpha. Only the support vectors determine the boundary; deleting any other point leaves the trained SVM unchanged, which is why the solution is sparse.

The hard-margin SVM demands every point be correctly classified with margin at least 1, which is infeasible when classes overlap. The soft margin (Cortes & Vapnik, 1995) adds slack variables and a penalty C, allowing some violations in exchange for a usable, wider boundary.

The loss max(0, 1 - y·f(x)) used by the SVM, where y·f(x) is the signed margin. It is zero once a point is correctly classified with margin at least 1 (giving sparsity) and rises linearly for points inside the margin or misclassified; it is the convex surrogate that makes max-margin training tractable.

The observation that an SVM uses the data only through inner products, so replacing the dot product x·x' with a kernel K(x,x') = phi(x)·phi(x') trains the model in a high- or infinite-dimensional feature space without ever computing the coordinates phi(x). Mercer's theorem characterizes which functions are valid kernels.

The default nonlinear kernel K(x,x') = exp(-gamma·||x - x'||^2), a smooth measure of similarity that decays with distance and corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature map. The width gamma controls reach: small gamma gives a smooth, near-linear boundary; large gamma wraps the boundary tightly around individual points and overfits.

The soft-margin hyperparameter trading margin width against training-error tolerance. Large C punishes every violation harshly (narrow margin, low bias, high variance, approaching the hard margin); small C tolerates mistakes for a fatter, smoother margin (high bias, low variance). It is the standard regularization knob in disguise.

The Lagrangian reformulation of the SVM in terms of the per-point multipliers alpha, in which the data appears only as inner products and the support-vector sparsity emerges from the KKT conditions. Solving the dual is what makes the kernel trick possible and yields the decision function as a weighted vote over support vectors.

refs.cite: Cortes, C. & Vapnik, V. (1995). Support-Vector Networks. Machine Learning 20(3), 273-297.
Boser, B. E., Guyon, I. M. & Vapnik, V. N. (1992). A Training Algorithm for Optimal Margin Classifiers. Proceedings of COLT '92, 144-152.
Schölkopf, B. & Smola, A. J. (2002). Learning with Kernels: Support Vector Machines, Regularization, Optimization, and Beyond. MIT Press.
Burges, C. J. C. (1998). A Tutorial on Support Vector Machines for Pattern Recognition. Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery 2(2), 121-167.
Chang, C.-C. & Lin, C.-J. (2011). LIBSVM: A Library for Support Vector Machines. ACM TIST 2(3), 1-27.
Shalev-Shwartz, S., Singer, Y., Srebro, N. & Cotter, A. (2011). Pegasos: Primal Estimated Sub-Gradient Solver for SVM. Mathematical Programming 127(1), 3-30.
